title: 3.1 String Fundamentals
subtitle: String definition, storage, and basic operations

content.introduction: Strings are one of the most common linear data structures in software systems. This section covers what strings are, how they are stored in memory, and how basic operations affect correctness and performance.

sections.title: 3.1.1 String Definition and Characteristics
sections.content:
Definition: A string is a finite sequence of characters arranged in linear order. Characters can be letters, digits, symbols, or Unicode code points.

Core Characteristics:
1) Ordered sequence: character positions matter.
2) Length-aware: each string has a defined length.
3) Character set dependent: ASCII, UTF-8, UTF-16, etc.
4) Immutable or mutable by language design.

String as a Linear Structure:
- Adjacent logical positions (index 0, 1, 2...) make strings a typical linear structure.
- Many algorithms depend on sequential access and indexing.
examples: "data-structures" can be represented with Unicode code points

sections.title: 3.1.2 Storage Models and Memory Representation
sections.content:
Common Storage Models:
1) Fixed-length arrays reserve a constant buffer size.
2) Dynamic arrays allow resizing as content grows.
3) Linked representation stores characters in linked nodes (less common in modern systems).

Encoding and Memory:
- ASCII uses 1 byte for standard English characters.
- UTF-8 uses variable length and is space-efficient for ASCII-heavy text.
- UTF-16 often used internally in many runtimes.

Practical Trade-offs:
- Contiguous storage improves cache locality and random index access.
- Linked storage simplifies some insert/delete operations but increases pointer overhead.
- Encoding choice affects length calculation and substring indexing semantics.
examples: C-style string uses char array ending with null terminator
Java String stores characters in internal arrays with encoding metadata
UTF-8 string length in bytes may differ from character count

sections.title: 3.1.3 Basic String Operations
sections.content:
Frequent Operations:
1) Access: read character by index.
2) Traversal: iterate over each character.
3) Comparison: lexicographical or equality check.
4) Concatenation: combine multiple strings.
5) Substring: extract a contiguous segment.
6) Insertion/Deletion/Replacement: modify content (or create new string for immutable types).

Complexity Overview (typical contiguous model):
- Index access: O(1)
- Traversal: O(n)
- Comparison: O(min(n, m))
- Concatenation: O(n + m) unless optimized by builders
- Substring copy: O(k)

Best Practices:
- Use string builders/buffers for repeated concatenation.
- Understand immutability to avoid hidden allocation costs.
- Be explicit about character encoding in I/O boundaries.
examples: Comparing two equal long strings may scan all characters
Repeated + in loops can cause quadratic allocation overhead
Substring extraction in UTF-8 should respect character boundaries

keyTakeaways: String is a classic linear structure organized by character positions
Storage model and encoding directly affect performance and correctness
Basic operations have predictable complexity patterns
Immutability is safe but may introduce extra memory allocation
Efficient string handling requires choosing proper APIs for the workload
